segments.s459-g81: بگفت is an archaic/epic past-tense verb form meaning 'he said' (ب- prefix + گفت) and here introduces reported speech or a remark; این is a demonstrative 'this' functioning as the object of بگفت. The coordinating و links that clause to the following clause. باد is the subject of the second clause ('a wind'), از جگر is an ablative phrase 'from the liver/heart/breast' showing origin, and برکشید is a past tense, third-person singular verb 'lifted/rose up' describing the wind's action. Word order is Verb‑Object for the first clause (بگفت این) then Subject‑Adverbial‑Verb for the second (باد از جگر برکشید).
segments.s459-g82: شد is the simple past copula/verb 'became' indicating a change of state. آن برگ گلنار is the subject noun phrase 'that pomegranate petal/leaf' (آن = 'that'; برگ گلنار = 'pomegranate leaf/petal' with the noun + modifier order). چون is a simile preposition 'like/as', and شنبلید is a noun meaning a seed‑spike/ear used as the comparison target. The clause therefore says the petal changed so that it resembled an ear/spike of grain.
segments.s459-g73: بگفت is a past third-person verb 'he said'. این‌که is a subordinating particle 'that' introducing reported content. دارای is an adjectival/participial root 'having, possessing' describing someone. داراکنون (poetic fusion of دارا + اکنون) means 'who now has / at present' and completes the description begun by دارای.
segments.s459-g74: شما را is the object/topic 'you' with the definite-object marker را placed before the predicate for emphasis. به نیکی is a prepositional phrase 'toward goodness / in goodness'. بود is the past copula 'was', linking to the noun رهنمون 'guide' which functions as the predicate noun.
segments.s459-g75: همه گوش دارید is an idiom literally 'you have all ears' meaning 'listen attentively' — دارید is present second-person plural 'you have'. و links the clauses. فرمان کنید is an imperative/request: فرمان (the order/command) with کنید (imperative 'do' / 'obey') indicating 'obey the command'.
segments.s459-g76: ز فرمان او is a prepositional phrase 'from/by his command' (ز = 'from/because of', او = 'his'). رامش جان کنید is a verbal phrase where رامش 'tranquility/solace' and جان 'soul' form the object and کنید (imperative) means 'make' or 'grant' — idiomatically 'let his command calm/comfort your soul.'
segments.s459-g77: که here introduces reported or explanatory content 'that / for'. این تخت شاهی is the noun phrase 'this royal throne / this kingship-seat'. نماند is past negative/negation of 'remain' (third-person) meaning 'did not remain / does not last'. دراز (postpositive) qualifies duration 'long' — poetic word order places it after the verb.
segments.s459-g78: به خوشی is an adverbial phrase 'in happiness / with joy'. رود is a verbal form 'go' (here future/subjunctive sense 'will go' in context) — note the provided lemma senses did not list the verbal meaning. زود 'soon' modifies the verb. خوانند is third-person plural present/subjunctive 'they call/summon', and باز 'again' follows it; together indicating 'they will call him back.'
segments.s459-g79: بکوشید is an imperative/optative 'strive/try' addressed to listeners. تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'. مهر و داد are coordinated nouns 'love (kindness) and justice'. آورید is a polite imperative/subjunctive 'bring' — together: 'Strive so that you bring love and justice.'
segments.s459-g80: به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for (your) joy'. مرا (here written as م) is the object 'me' but the provided lemma/senses do not include that meaning, so it's marked unavailable. را is the object marker attached to مرا, نیز 'also', یاد آورید is the verb phrase 'bring to mind / remember' (آورید 'bring/mention' with یاد 'remembrance'), meaning 'also remember me.'
segments.s459-g1: شبی is a sentence‑initial temporal adverb meaning “one night.” خفته is a past participle/adjectival use meaning “sleeping” (here describing ماه). بد before ماه is an epithet “bad/ill‑omened”; in classical Persian epithets often precede the noun. با شهریار places the moon with the king (با = “with”), so the whole chunk reads literally “one night the moon sleeping ill with the king.”
segments.s459-g2: پر از is the fixed phrase “full of” (پر = “full (of)”, از = the partitive/of phrase). The following nouns are a linked list of qualities: گوهر (jewel), بوی (scent), رنگ (colour), نگار (here best read as the beautified/ornamental/‘beloved’ sense used poetically). The connector و simply strings the items together.
segments.s459-g3: همانا is an emphatic particle “verily/indeed.” که introduces the subordinate clause here with the sense “when/that;” برزد is an archaic perfective verb meaning “struck/occurred” (i.e. a moment came). یکی تیز دم = “a single/one sharp moment” (یکی = one/a certain, تیز = sharp/swift, دم = moment/breath).
segments.s459-g4: شهنشاه = “the emperor” (title). زان = “from that / because of that” (here pointing back to the instant). تیز دم repeats the phrase “the sharp moment.” شد is the past of شدن “became.” دژم is an adjectival predicate “sullen/grim.” The clause thus says the emperor became sullen from that instant.
segments.s459-g5: بپیچید is a past form of پیچیدن “to wrap/turn” here meaning “he wrapped (himself).” در جامه = “in/into the garment/robe.” و links the actions. سر بتافت is an idiomatic, slightly archaic expression where بتافت (from تافتن) means “turned” or “turned toward,” so “and he turned his head.”
segments.s459-g6: که introduces the reason (“for/because that”). از = “from/out of.” نکهتش is a genitive form “his/her fragrance/perfume” (نکهت = fragrance + possessive -ش). بوی = “smell/scent.” ناخوش is adjectival “unpleasant” (here describing the scent). بیافت (an old form of افتادن/بیافتن) is used here intransitively “falls/comes forth” — i.e. an unpleasant scent issued/fell from that perfume.
segments.s459-g7: ازان بوی = “from that scent/thereupon” (ازان = from that). شد is “became.” شاه ایران = “the king of Iran.” دژم is the predicate adjective “sullen/gloomy.” The clause states cause (from that scent) and effect (the king became gloomy).
segments.s459-g8: پراندیشه is a compound adjective meaning “full of thought / pensive” (پر‑ = full + اندیشه = thought). جان here means “soul/life” and is the noun governed by the adjective (i.e. “soul full of thought”). ابروان پر ز خم is a parallel descriptive phrase: ابروان = “eyebrows,” پر = “full (of),” ز is the poetic preposition “of/from,” and خم = “curve/arch.” Together: “his soul pensive, his brows full of curve/arch (i.e. arched brows).”
segments.s459-g33: نیاورد is a past-tense negative verb meaning 'did not bring/utter.' The clause has a verb-first, object-after feel: نیاورد (did not utter) + کس (anyone/no one) + نام دارا (the name 'Dara'). به بر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of' (به + بر), marking the sphere in which the name was not brought up.
segments.s459-g34: This is a nominal (equational) sentence with بود as the past copula: 'سکندر پسر بود' = 'Alexander was the son.' The conjunction و links an elided copula onto the following nominal 'قیصر پدر' = '(and) Caesar (was) the father.' Word order is subject + predicate + بود; the second predicate omits بود by ellipsis.
segments.s459-g35: همی is an adverbial particle giving a continuous/instantaneous coloring (here marking the experiencing of shame). ننگش آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase literally 'shame came to him' = 'he was ashamed' (ننگ + possessive -ش + آمد). که introduces the content clause. گفتی is the second-person past tense 'you said/told,' and به کس is the indirect object 'to anyone.'
segments.s459-g36: This subordinate clause explains what was said. که = 'that.' دارا is the subject (proper name). ز is the poetic equivalent of از meaning 'of/from' linking دارا to فرزند من ('of my offspring'). کرد is the past verb 'made/did' used with بس ('enough') to mean 'made sufficient' or idiomatically 'was enough/was sufficient.' بس is an adverb/adjective meaning 'enough' and completes the predicate.
segments.s459-g37: بر آخر is a locative/time phrase 'at the end' or 'finally.' یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one.' مادیان (mare) is the noun, followed by the adjectives بد (bad/ill‑tempered) and بلند (tall). Persian places adjectives after the noun, and the phrase describes a particular mare brought at the end.
segments.s459-g38: که is a relative pronoun 'which/that,' referring back to the mare. کارزاری is an adjective meaning 'martial' or 'fit for a campaign.' و connects to زیبا 'beautiful/fine,' and سمند is 'steed/warhorse' (here indicating a high-quality warhorse). The adjectives qualify the mare ('a warlike and beautiful steed').
segments.s459-g39: همان شب is a temporal phrase 'that very same night.' یکی is 'a certain.' کره‌ای here denotes 'a foal/young horse' (the lexicon entry given doesn't show this sense, so the sense index is unavailable). زاد is the past verb 'was born' (or 'gave birth'), used impersonally here to report the birth. خنگ is an adjective meaning 'stupid/dull' describing the newborn foal.
segments.s459-g40: برش here functions as بر + enclitic -ش 'on/onto it' (i.e., 'on it' or 'about it'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as,' and the following بر شیر gives the comparison 'like (that of) a lion' (i.e., lionlike in some feature). و links the final descriptive adjectives کوتاه 'short' and لنگ 'lame/limping.' Together this gives three more qualities of the foal: lionlike in some aspect, short, and limping.
segments.s459-g57: This is a relative clause describing 'هنرها' (the arts). Word order places the head noun 'هنرها' before the clause introduced by 'که' ('that/which'). 'باشد' is a present/subjunctive copular verb meaning 'be' (here: 'that are'), and 'کیان را' is marked with the object/topic marker 'را' to indicate the beneficiaries ('to kings'). 'به کار' literally 'to/for use' qualifies which arts are meant: 'useful to kings.'
segments.s459-g58: 'سکندر بیاموخت' is a simple past clause: 'Alexander learned.' 'بیاموخت' is an active past verb here. The preposition 'ز' (poetic = 'from') introduces the source 'آموزگار' (the teacher), so the whole segment means 'Alexander learned from the teacher.'
segments.s459-g59: 'تو گفتی' is a 2nd-person past reporting ('you said'); the lexical senses supplied for 'گفتی' did not include the verbal past meaning, so its sense is marked -1. 'نشاید' is an impersonal negative/formal expression meaning 'it is not fitting/it must not (be)' and 'مگر' introduces the exception 'except'; 'داد را' ('justice') is marked by 'را' as the focused object — together: 'you said it is not fitting except by justice.'
segments.s459-g60: 'وگر' here introduces an alternative ('or if / or else'). 'تخت شاهی' is a compound where 'تخت' (throne) is qualified by 'شاهی' (kingship/royal), yielding 'the royal throne' or 'throne of kings.' The conjunction 'و' links 'تخت شاهی' with 'بنیاد' (foundation), and 'را' again marks the object/focus; the segment offers the alternative means: 'or (only) by the throne of kings and the foundation.'
segments.s459-g61: 'وزان پس' is a poetic compounded temporal phrase meaning 'and thereafter / from that time on.' The clause 'که ناهید نزد پدر' ('that Anahita to/near the father') uses 'که' as a relative/temporal marker; 'نزد پدر' means 'in the presence of / to the father' (prepositional phrase following the name).
segments.s459-g62: 'بیامد' is past 'came.' The sequence 'زنی خواست' means 'she asked for a woman' (i.e., requested a woman/bride). 'دارا دگر' follows as beneficiary + modifier: 'دارا' appears as the person for whom the woman is requested (here a proper name/recipient), and 'دگر' (= 'another') modifies 'زنی'. Note: the lexicon entries provided for 'دارا' did not include the proper-name reading, so its sense is marked -1; the phrase as a whole functions 'she asked for another woman for Dārā.'
segments.s459-g63: 'یکی' here is an indefinite 'a certain / a' introducing 'کودک' ('a child'). 'آمدش' is past 'came' with the enclitic pronoun '-ش' = 'to him' (came to him). 'با فر و یال' ('with فر and mane') lists poetic attributes: 'فر' is the royal radiance/glory and 'یال' (mane) is a heraldic/poetic attribute; the preposition 'با' links them as accompanying features.
segments.s459-g64: 'ز' is the poetic 'from/of' introducing parentage: 'ز فرزند ناهید' = 'from Anahita's child/offspring.' 'کهتر به سال' is a comparative phrase: 'کهتر' = 'smaller / inferior' used idiomatically as 'younger,' and 'به سال' = 'in years,' so together 'younger in years.' Word order places the comparative phrase after the noun it modifies, as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s459-g17: بشد is a past/preterite form meaning 'became' or 'happened' here; the clause says a state befell two terms in juxtaposition: بوی و کامش ('his scent and his desire/appetite'). بسوخت is a past passive/active-stative meaning 'was burned/consumed' and attaches to کامش as its predicate. Word order is Subjectless/state + objects: 'بشد (it happened) [that] ناخوشی (illness) [befell] بوی و کامش; بسوخت (his desire was burned)'.
segments.s459-g18: به here functions adverbially 'in the manner of / like' (i.e. 'as'); کردار is used in the sense of 'manner/conduct' (a poetic use meaning 'way, fashion'); دیبا is the simile term 'brocade'; رخش in this poetic line means 'countenance/face' rather than a horse; برفروخت is a past verb meaning 'kindled / inflamed' (metaphorically 'flushed/blazed'). The whole phrase reads 'his face kindled like brocade.'
segments.s459-g19: اگر چند is a concessive opening 'although / even if somewhat'; چند here has a moderating sense 'somewhat / a little'; مشکین is used poetically as 'musk-scented / perfumed' (rather than merely 'dark'); شد is the past 'became'; آن خوب‌چهر 'that fair-faced one' is the subject. Word order is standard poetic: concessive particle + degree + adjective + copula.
segments.s459-g20: دژم شد is 'became sullen/grim' (predicate first as is common in Persian poetic diction). دلارای را uses را as the definite/affected-object marker: 'with respect to Dilara' (i.e. his feeling toward Dilara). جای مهر is an ablative/locative phrase 'in place of love / instead of affection' — together: 'the place of love for Dilara became sullen.'
segments.s459-g21: دل پادشا is a genitival phrase 'the king's heart' (دل + possessor). سرد گشت is a compound verbal phrase 'became cold' (گشت = became/turned). از here introduces the object/target of the emotional change 'with respect to / toward the bride' (a common Persian use: 'cold from/with the bride' = 'cold toward the bride').
segments.s459-g22: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'sent / dispatched' with an implicit subject (the king). بازش is باز + enclitic pronoun ش (her) and here means 'her back / back again' (i.e. 'sent her back'); بر governs the indirect destination 'to / toward' and فیلقوس is a proper name as the destination. The sequence thus expresses 'he sent her back to Filqus.'
segments.s459-g23: This is a noun phrase: غمی دختر و کودک 'the grief/sorrow of the daughter and the child'. اندر نهان is a locative/adverbial 'in secret / hidden within'. The order places the abstract noun (غمی) first, followed by its possessors, then the adverbial phrase describing how it was kept.
segments.s459-g24: نگفت is the negative past 'did not say' (the printed senses list given is for 'گفت' as a noun; here the verb is negative past—no listed sense exactly matches, so index -1 is used). آن سخن 'that speech/matter' is the object; با کسی 'to anyone/with anyone' uses با + کسی; در جهان 'in the world' is a limiting adverbial. Overall: 'she did not tell that matter to anyone in the world.'
segments.s459-g9: SOV order with explicit object marker را: پزشکان (subject, plural) + داننده را (definite/known object) + خواندند (past, 3rd person plural). خواندند here is the past-tense verb 'they called/summoned' (an older poetic form with -ند). داننده is a post‑nominal epithet 'the knowledgeable/expert one' functioning as the direct object.
segments.s459-g10: به نزدیک ناهید is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near Nahid' (به + location word نزدیک); بنشاندند is past, 3rd person plural 'they seated/placed (him)'. The object (the man) is carried over from the previous clause; the verb is transitive and places him near Nahid.
segments.s459-g11: یکی مرد = 'a certain man' (یکی as an indefinite determiner before the noun). The following coordinated adjectives بینادل و نیک‌رای modify مرد; they are post‑nominal adjectival/epithetic phrases joined by و. بینادل is an archaic/uncertain word here; نیک‑رای is a compound adjective meaning 'well‑disposed/with good judgment'.
segments.s459-g12: پژوهید is a past tense verb (classical/poetic form) meaning 'he examined/searched' (3rd person). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until ...' whose verb is آمد. دارو آمد به جای is the subordinate clause 'the medicine came into place / arrived (and was ready)'; به جای here is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'into its place' meaning 'was delivered/available'.
segments.s459-g13: Relative clause introduced by که modifies گیاهی: 'a plant that ...'. سوزنده کام is a noun phrase lit. 'burning of desire' (i.e., 'that inflames desire'); بود is the past copula 'was'. The predicate describes the plant's effect (an aphrodisiac).
segments.s459-g14: An adverbial/location phrase: به روم اندر = 'in Rome' (classical word order). اسکندرش is the possessive/suffixed form 'of Alexander' (‑ش = his/of him). نام بود is a verbless/periphrastic clause 'had the name' = 'was called'. Together: 'in Rome it had Alexander's name' (i.e., was called 'Alexander').
segments.s459-g15: بمالید is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they rubbed / smeared / anointed' (the remedy is implied as the object). Two prepositional phrases follow with repeated بر: بر کام او = 'on her/his desire' (او refers back to the patient) and بر پزشک functions adverbially here ('before/in the presence of the physician' or 'in front of the physician'); the repetition of بر links two targets/locations for the action. The exact referent of او is determined by context (a nearby person).
segments.s459-g16: ببارید is an intransitive past 'fell' (poetic use like 'drops fell'). چندی modifies duration 'for a while'. ز introduces the source 'from': ز مژگان سرشک = 'drops (سرشک) from the eyelashes (مژگان)'. The clause describes teardrops falling from the lashes for a short time.
segments.s459-g25: چو (an introductory conjunction) here means 'when' and introduces a temporal clause. نه ماه بگذشت is a past tense clause: ماه (month) with نه (nine) is the duration, and بگذشت is the past form 'passed' (time passed). بر خوب‌چهر places that passing 'upon/over the fair‑faced (woman)'—بر marks the locus/target and خوب‌چهر is a compound adjective-noun 'fair‑faced'.
segments.s459-g26: یکی کودک آمد is a simple past narrative: یکی 'a certain' (here indefinite article 'a'), کودک 'infant/child', آمد 'came/arrived' (i.e. was born/appeared). چو تابنده مهر is a simile introduced by چو 'like/as', تابنده 'shining/resplendent' modifies مهر; مهر is used poetically as a radiant figure (here 'sun' or radiant presence).
segments.s459-g27: This is an elliptical/poetic clause giving origin or association: ز 'from' lists names/places (بالا, اروند) and the following بویا here functions as an archaic copular/preserving element ('was' in older diction) that links the list to برش. برش functions as a nominal 'her (lineage/condition/being upon her)'—the line uses inversion and omission common in poetry, so literal order is compressed.
segments.s459-g28: سکندر همی خواندی مادرش is another inverted poetic clause: همی + past خواندی gives a customary/habitual meaning ('used to call / would call'). مادرش 'her mother' is the agent (placed after the verb for poetic word order) with the pronominal suffix ش = 'her'. The verb خواند here means 'to name/call (someone)'.
segments.s459-g29: بفرخ is an optative/formulaic verb meaning 'may it prosper' or 'may it flourish' and stands alone as a benediction. همی داشت آن نام را is a habitual past construction: همی + داشت indicates she 'constantly had/kept' that name (آن نام), with را marking the name as definite object.
segments.s459-g30: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from which' referring back to that name; یافت is simple past 'found/obtained' (here 'gained' or 'obtained by means of it'). از ناخوشی کام را literally 'from the illness/unhappiness of desire' — ناخوشی modifies کام and را marks کام as the definite object of یافت; the whole says she obtained relief/fulfillment of desire from that which had been troubling her.
segments.s459-g31: همی گفت قیصر is habitual past: همی + گفت gives 'would say / used to say' and قیصر is the speaker (subject). به هر مهتری marks the addressee/indirect object 'to every chief/preeminent one' (به + هر + مهتری). Note the poetical placement of elements.
segments.s459-g32: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that': پیدا شد is a light verb phrase 'is found / is discovered' (پیدا 'apparent/found' + شد 'became/was'), meaning 'whoever turns out to be'. از تخم من is a genitive/explanatory phrase 'from my seed/stock' and قیصری is a predicative adjective/noun 'Caesarian/imperial' (i.e. 'is a Caesar'); the copula sense is supplied by شد/context.
segments.s459-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; زاینده is a noun 'the begetter/producer' governing قیصر (a genitive: 'of the Caesar'). برافراخت is a past tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'raised/erected' (ب + افراخت), and یال is the direct object 'mane/crest' placed after the verb — a typical Persian word order in poetry (Prepositional phrase → verb → object).
segments.s459-g42: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'); آن is the demonstrative 'that' referring back to the birth; زادنش is زادن + enclitic ـش meaning 'its/their bearing/that birth' (the verbal noun used as a noun with a possessive/third‑person suffix); فرخ is an adjective 'fortunate/auspicious'; آمد is simple past 'came/occurred' and together آمد به فال is the idiom 'proved an omen' where به + فال = 'as an omen'.
segments.s459-g43: به شبگیر is an adverbial phrase 'at/into the night' (به + شبگیر 'night‑time/nocturnal'), فرزند را is the definite object (را marking the direct object 'the child'), and خواستی is past 2nd‑person singular 'you wanted/asked for' (simple past). The clause therefore reads 'you desired/asked for the child at night.'
segments.s459-g44: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same'; مادیان را (mare + object marker را) is the direct object; بیاراستی is a past 2nd‑person form meaning 'you arranged/prepared/trimmed' (a poetic/archaic verb form from آراستن with a prefix), so the clause means 'you prepared that mare.'
segments.s459-g45: بسودی is an adverb/prepositional phrase meaning 'for the benefit/advantage' (to good effect). همان کره را is 'that foal' marked as definite object with را, and چشم و یال lists two features 'eye and mane' — the whole phrase implies giving/setting those features 'for the foal's advantage.'
segments.s459-g46: که again introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'; همتای is a noun 'equal/counterpart'; اسکندر names Alexander; او is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'he' referring to the foal; بد به سال is a short adjectival phrase 'bad/ill with respect to years' — i.e., 'ill‑matched in age' or 'not comparable in years' (bad = poorly, به سال = in years). The clause explains the comparison with Alexander in respect to age.
segments.s459-g47: سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the subject; اندرین (archaic) means 'in these' or 'among these'; نیز 'also' adds emphasis; چندی is an adverb 'for a while/some time'; بگشت here is a past verb 'passed/elapsed' in the sense 'time passed' — overall: 'some time also passed in these heavens.'
segments.s459-g48: ز is the preposition 'from/of'; هرگونه‌ای means 'of every kind/whatever sort'; سالیان is the plural noun 'years'; برگذشت is a past verb 'passed by/elapsed'. Together the phrase says 'years of every kind passed by.'
segments.s459-g65: همان روز (lit. “that same day”) is a temporal phrase placed before the verb. داراش کردند نام is a causative phrasing: کردند (past, 3rd pl.) + نام (name) — literally “they made (gave) the name Dārāš.” In older/poetic Persian the name (داراش) can appear directly in this slot without a linking preposition.
segments.s459-g66: که تا ... باشد به کام is a purposive/optative clause: که تا = “so that / in order that,” باشد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning “may be” or “might be,” and به کام = “to the liking / into the satisfaction.” از پدر بیش is a comparative phrase (“more than/from the father”), so the whole clause means “so that he might be more pleasing than (or more to) his father.”
segments.s459-g67: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” ده سال بگذشت is a simple past clause (“ten years passed”). زین با دو سال is an adverbial addition meaning roughly “with two more years” or “and two years beyond that”; زین here is an archaic/poetic element used to indicate ‘this/that additional amount.’ The time expression precedes the verb as is typical in older Persian.
segments.s459-g68: شکست اندر آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: شکست (a noun meaning misfortune/defeat) + اندر آمد (“came in/on” = befell). The following prepositional complements به سال و به مال indicate the spheres affected — “in his years (health/age)” and “in his wealth (possessions).”
segments.s459-g69: بپژمرد is a past (poetic) form meaning “withered / wasted away.” The following name phrase داراب پور همای is an appositive/genitive: داراب (name) + پور همای = “Darab, son of Homay.” Word order here places the verb before the proper-name phrase (a feature common in poetic narration).
segments.s459-g70: همی is a classical adverb often marking continuous or repeated action (“kept on / then”). خواندندش is past plural with the enclitic object -ش (him): it can mean “they called/summoned him” or “they named him” depending on context; here the sense is “they summoned/called him.” به دیگر سرای is a prepositional phrase “to another house/abode/court.” Note that سرای here is the noun “abode/house” rather than any singing-related sense.
segments.s459-g71: بزرگان و فرزانگان را بخواند: the nouns بزرگان و فرزانگان (“the great ones and the wise/sages”) are marked by the object marker را and are the direct object of بخواند (past “he summoned/called”). In narrative Persian older forms often put the object before the verb.
segments.s459-g72: ز تخت = “from (or out of) the throne”; بزرگی here means “greatness” or “loftiness,” and فراوان is an adverb/adjective ‘abundant’ modifying the result. براند is an older narrative verb form (from راندن) — here used in a distributive/active sense (“he dispensed / bestowed / set forth / drove out” depending on nuance). Taken together the phrase says he issued/dispensed great abundance from the throne (i.e., conferred high rank/greatness).
segments.s459-g49: سکندر is the subject (proper name). دل ... گرفت is an idiom: literally “the heart was seized,” meaning he became filled with a feeling; خسروانی modifies دل (“a kingly/royal heart” or “kingly desire”). گرفت is a past-tense verb here functioning intransitively ('was seized' / 'became seized by').
segments.s459-g50: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun (infinitive) meaning ‘speaking’ or ‘to speak’ and is the thing that seizes him; پهلوانی qualifies the manner/content ('in a heroic/heroic-style way'); گرفت again is past ‘was seized / took to’ and shows he adopted that habit or mood.
segments.s459-g51: فزون از + noun expresses 'more than / exceeding' (here 'more than a son'). داشتی is a past-tense form 'had' (poetically used here), and قیصرش is 'his Caesar' (قیصر + possessive -ش) serving as the subject whose possession is asserted. Word order is the classical SOV/poetic ordering.
segments.s459-g52: بیاراستی is a verbal form from آراستن/بیاراستن meaning 'adorned / equipped / made' (here indicating someone was made or endowed with something). پهلوانی is the object/quality given ('heroism, prowess'); برش is an archaic contracted form functioning as an indirect-object pronoun ('for him' / 'to him'). Together: '(someone) made/arrayed him with heroism.'
segments.s459-g53: خرد یافت is a past clause 'he found/gained wisdom' (یافت = 'obtained/found'); لختی is an adverbial 'a short while' or 'a little,' modifying یافت. و شد کاردان links with a new predicate: شد 'became' + کاردان 'skilled/expert' (predicate adjective).
segments.s459-g54: This is a string of coordinated qualities. هشیوار 'alert/aware' is a predicate adjective; و introduces the next phrase; با + سنگ uses با to mark the instrument/means or domain of skill ('with stone(s)' i.e. skilled with stones, likely stone-throwing); و connects to بسیاردان 'very learned' (intensive adjective).
segments.s459-g55: ولی عهد is a fixed nominal phrase meaning 'crown prince' or 'heir apparent' (compound). گشت is past 'became.' از پس + name means 'after' (in sequence or succession), so از پس فیلقوس locates the succession: 'became heir after Filqus.'
segments.s459-g56: بدیدار 'on/at meeting' or 'at the sight/visit' is an adverbial phrase here (to/at the meeting); او is the pronoun 'him' (the person met). داشتی is past 'he had' (possessed/received). نعم و بوس are coordinated nouns: نعم = 'blessings/bounties/gifts' and بوس = 'kisses' (acts of greeting). The phrase says that at the meeting he had/received gifts and kisses.

sentences.s459-s41: He said this, and a wind rose from his breast; that pomegranate petal became like an ear of grain.
sentences.s459-s37: He said that the one who now possesses (wealth) was your guide to goodness.
sentences.s459-s38: Be all ears and obey; let his command bring calm to your soul.
sentences.s459-s39: For this royal throne will not remain long; he will soon go in happiness, and they will call him back.
sentences.s459-s40: Strive so that you bring love and justice; also remember me in your joy.
sentences.s459-s1: One night the moon lay sleeping ill-omened with the king, full of jewels, scent, color and beauty.
sentences.s459-s2: Truly, when a swift moment struck, the emperor — from that sharp instant — became sullen.
sentences.s459-s3: He wrapped himself in his robe and turned his head, for from her perfume an unpleasant scent arose.
sentences.s459-s4: From that scent the king of Iran became gloomy: his soul full of thought, his brows full of arching curve.
sentences.s459-s17: No one uttered the name 'Dara' before him; Alexander was the son and Caesar the father.
sentences.s459-s18: He was ashamed that you had told anyone that ‘Dara, of my offspring, was enough.’
sentences.s459-s19: At the end there was a certain mare, tall and ill‑tempered — martial and a handsome steed.
sentences.s459-s20: That same night a foal was born, dull of wit; on it (it had) a lionlike (appearance), and it was short and lame.
sentences.s459-s29: Alexander learned from his teacher the arts that are of use to kings.
sentences.s459-s30: You said it would not be fitting except by justice, or else by the royal throne and its foundation.
sentences.s459-s31: And after that, when Anahita came to the father, she asked (for) another woman for Dārā.
sentences.s459-s32: A child came to him with royal radiance and a mane — from Anahita's offspring, younger in years.
sentences.s459-s9: Illness came upon his scent and appetite; his desire was burned, and his countenance kindled like brocade.
sentences.s459-s10: Although that fair-faced one became somewhat musk‑scented, the place of love for Dilara grew sullen.
sentences.s459-s11: The king's heart grew cold toward the bride, and he sent her back to Filqus.
sentences.s459-s12: She kept the sorrow for the daughter and the child hidden; she did not tell that matter to anyone in the world.
sentences.s459-s5: The physicians summoned the knowledgeable man and seated him near Nahid.
sentences.s459-s6: A certain, discerning, well-minded man examined and searched until the medicine arrived in place.
sentences.s459-s7: A plant that kindled desire — in Rome it bore Alexander's name.
sentences.s459-s8: They anointed (smeared) it on her desire; in front of the physician, for a while teardrops fell from the eyelashes.
sentences.s459-s13: When nine months had passed over the fair‑faced woman, a child came forth like the shining sun.
sentences.s459-s14: From Bālā and Arvand and Buya was her origin; her mother would call her 'Sikandar.'
sentences.s459-s15: May it prosper — she always bore that name; by it she found relief from her unhappy longing.
sentences.s459-s16: The Caesar would always say to every chief: 'Whoever is found to be from my seed is a Caesar.'
sentences.s459-s21: From the Caesar’s begetter he raised a mane; that birth came as a fortunate omen.
sentences.s459-s22: At nightfall you desired the child; you made that same mare ready.
sentences.s459-s23: You fashioned that same foal’s eyes and mane to good effect, for he was not matched by Alexander in years.
sentences.s459-s24: Some time also passed in these heavens; years of every kind went by.
sentences.s459-s33: That same day they gave him the name Dārāš, so that he might be more to his father's liking.
sentences.s459-s34: When ten years had passed — and two more years besides — misfortune befell him in his years and in his possessions.
sentences.s459-s35: Dārāb, son of Homāy, wasted away; they then summoned him to another abode.
sentences.s459-s36: He summoned the great and the wise, and from the throne he bestowed abundant greatness.
sentences.s459-s25: Alexander was seized by a kingly longing, and he took to speaking in a heroic manner.
sentences.s459-s26: The Caesar had more than one son; (and) you made him heroic / you furnished him with heroism.
sentences.s459-s27: He gained some wisdom briefly, and became skilled; alert, expert with the stone, and very learned.
sentences.s459-s28: He became crown-prince after Filqus; at his meeting with him he had gifts and kisses.
